---
title: "P1: Download + Intro to Python & VS Code"
author: "Nicky Wakim"
title-slide-attributes:
    data-background-color: "#3e9c77"
date: "09/30/2024"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: "../simple_NW_python.scss"
    chalkboard: true
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    width: 1955
    height: 1100
    footer: Python 01 Slides
    html-math-method: mathjax
    highlight-style: arrow
execute:
  echo: true
  freeze: auto
---

## How did I make these slides?

- I used Gemini! [Here is my exchange with Gemini](https://gemini.google.com/share/81f916269cfe)

## Activities for the class

- Every other week, you will transform the R lectures to Python lectures! 
  - This is pretty loose: within the topic, you can focus on whatever you'd like

## What is Python?

::: columns
::: {.column width="70%"}
- A general-purpose programming language
- The "gold standard" for machine learning and data science
- Extensive libraries for biostatistics (`pandas`, `statsmodels`)
- Highly readable syntax 
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
![](./images/Python-logo-notext.png)
:::
:::



## What is VS Code? (Our RStudio equivalent)

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Python is the Engine**

- It does the calculations.
- You *could* run it in a terminal, but it’s not pretty.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**VS Code is the Dashboard**

- An Integrated Development Environment (IDE).
- It provides a script editor, file explorer, and data viewer all in one place.
:::
:::



## We open VS Code on our computer

- Just like in R, we rarely open the "Python" app itself.
- We open **Visual Studio Code** (or Anaconda Navigator) to write and run our code.
- Inside VS Code, we will use **Jupyter Notebooks** (.ipynb files)—these are the Python equivalent of Quarto or RMarkdown.

## If you have issues downloading on your laptop

- If your installation is giving you trouble, there is a powerful browser-based version provided by Google.
- **Google Colab**: <https://colab.research.google.com/>
    - Works just like a Google Doc for code.
    - No installation required.
    - Great for following along in class today!

## So let's take some time to download each!

- **Step 1: Install Python**
    - Link: <https://www.python.org/downloads/>
    - **CRITICAL:** On Windows, check the box that says **"Add Python to PATH"** during installation.

- **Step 2: Install VS Code**
    - Link: <https://code.visualstudio.com/>
    - Once installed, open VS Code, go to the "Extensions" view (square icons on the left), and search for/install the **"Python" extension** by Microsoft.

- **Alternative (The All-in-One):**
    - You can also download **Anaconda**: <https://www.anaconda.com/>
    - This installs Python, VS Code, and most math libraries all at once.

## VS Code Anatomy (for Python)

- **Editor (Top Left):** Where you write your scripts or Notebook cells.
- **Variables/Jupyter Server (Top Right):** Keeps track of your data frames and variables.
- **Terminal/Output (Bottom):** Where Python "talks" back to you.
- **File Explorer (Far Left):** To manage your datasets and folders.
